intro.title: Empire of Four
intro.body: Diocletian's tetrarchy divided the empire among four rulers, each based at a forward capital near the threatened frontiers. Nicomedia (East), Sirmium (Danube), Mediolanum (West), and Augusta Treverorum (Rhine) replaced Rome as working seats.

stops.name: Nicomedia
stops.note: Diocletian's eastern capital; he proclaimed himself emperor here in 284. Modern İzmit.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Bay-side imperial palace with porphyry columns, harbour beyond, basilica under construction.

stops.name: Antioch
stops.note: Eastern operations base during the Persian campaigns of 296-299. The mint here issued tetrarchic coinage.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Walled Syrian capital along the Orontes, palace on the river island, colonnaded street.

stops.name: Alexandria
stops.note: Diocletian besieged the usurper Domitius Domitianus here, 297-298. The 'Pompey's Pillar' column commemorates the eventual capitulation.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Lighthouse of Pharos in the distance, Egyptian-Greek port, victory column being raised.

stops.name: Salonae (Split)
stops.note: Diocletian's retirement palace, built 295-305 CE. The colonnaded peristyle and mausoleum survive as the core of modern Split.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Coastal palace-fortress with peristyle, mausoleum dome, Adriatic in the background.

stops.name: Sirmium
stops.note: Tetrarchic Danube capital. Site of repeated imperial conferences; modern Sremska Mitrovica.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Danube-side fortified city, four porphyry-mantled emperors meeting in a basilica forecourt.
